skill.name: polymarket
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through Polymarket API, trading, market data, and real-time WebSocket integration using bundled reference documentation.
skill.summary: Build and review Polymarket API, trading, and live market data workflows.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but review found the skill is primarily Markdown documentation and examples, not executable automation. Most command, URL, filesystem, and cryptography hits are false positives from code snippets and Polymarket terms, but the references include realistic private key, API credential, WebSocket, and trading examples that warrant a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Private Key Examples in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The references show examples that load a wallet private key and CLOB API credentials from environment variables and a .env file. This is legitimate for Polymarket development, but users could expose funds or account credentials if they paste secrets into prompts, logs, or shared workspaces.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference .env loading, process.env.PK, CLOB credential fields, and private key handling. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because these are documentation snippets with warnings, not hidden executable code.

medium_findings.title: Live Trading and Order Management Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill covers authenticated trading, order updates, and market data flows. A user following examples without review could place, cancel, or monitor real trades through Polymarket clients and WebSocket topics.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill scope and examples clearly include trading and authenticated data integration. I did not find evidence of automatic trade execution by the skill itself, so the issue is a user-action risk.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are triggered by fenced code blocks, inline commands, and TypeScript examples in documentation. I did not find evidence that the skill itself runs shell commands or dynamically executes user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible Markdown examples and installation snippets. No executable script or tool wrapper was found in the reviewed skill content.

low_findings.title: Expected External Network Endpoints in Reference Material
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings mostly identify official Polymarket, GitHub, and API endpoint references. Network access is inherent to a Polymarket market data skill, but users should verify endpoints before sending authenticated requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as documentation sources or package references, not concealed exfiltration destinations. The remaining risk is normal third-party API use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented .env and process.env Secret Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes reference examples for reading wallet and CLOB credentials from environment variables. This is a sensitive pattern and should be handled outside prompts and committed files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential names and .env loading are explicit. The pattern is documented rather than hidden, which lowers severity but not sensitivity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated WebSocket Subscription Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows CLOB user subscription authentication with key, secret, and passphrase placeholders. This is expected for private user data but requires careful secret handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The snippet is a direct authenticated subscription example. It uses placeholders, so there is no evidence of embedded real credentials.

content.value_statement: Polymarket integrations require correct API, trading, and WebSocket details. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured reference guidance for market data, live streams, and trading workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Polymarket API endpoints, market objects, events, tags, and activity data.
Guides WebSocket subscriptions for trades, comments, RFQ events, prices, and market updates.
Summarizes CLOB trading concepts, order flows, rate limits, and authentication requirements.
Helps draft TypeScript or Python integration plans using the bundled reference examples.
Identifies when private user data streams require API credentials and careful secret handling.
limitations: Does not provide official financial, legal, or trading advice.
Does not verify live market prices unless the assistant has separate network access.
Does not remove the need to secure private keys, API keys, and wallet permissions.
Reference content may lag behind Polymarket platform or API changes.

use_cases.title: Prototype Market Dashboards
use_cases.description: Plan a dashboard that reads Polymarket events, markets, prices, and activity feeds.

use_cases.title: Monitor Live Market Streams
use_cases.description: Design WebSocket subscriptions for trades, order book updates, comments, and crypto price feeds.

use_cases.title: Review Trading Integrations
use_cases.description: Check order placement, authentication, rate limits, and wallet handling before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Right Reference
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Polymarket skill to tell me which reference files I should read for market data, WebSocket streams, and CLOB trading.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Market Data App
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the Polymarket references, outline an app that lists events, shows markets, and refreshes prices. Include endpoints and data objects.

prompt_templates.title: Design WebSocket Subscriptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WebSocket subscription plan for live trades and price changes on selected Polymarket markets. Include filters and reconnect behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Trading Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned Polymarket CLOB trading workflow for authentication, private key handling, rate limits, and order lifecycle risks.

output_examples.input: I need live trade updates for one Polymarket event.
output_examples.output: A subscription plan naming the activity trades topic, event or market filters, reconnect handling, and message processing steps.

output_examples.input: Help me understand CLOB authentication before placing orders.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering wallet type, API credentials, passphrase handling, signature mode, allowances, and rate limit precautions.

output_examples.input: Which files explain market lookup and trading examples?
output_examples.output: A short map from API, getting started, trading, and WebSocket reference files to the user goals they support.

best_practices: Keep private keys and API secrets out of prompts, logs, source control, and shared transcripts.
Use filters and rate limits when subscribing to high-volume market or activity streams.
Test market data and order workflows in a controlled environment before using real funds.
anti_patterns: Pasting real wallet private keys or CLOB secrets into an assistant prompt.
Using wildcard WebSocket subscriptions when a narrow market or event filter is available.
Treating generated trading examples as financial advice or production-ready automation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps assistants answer Polymarket API, CLOB trading, market data, and WebSocket integration questions.

faq.question: Can it place trades for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation guidance and examples. Any trade execution needs separate tools and explicit user action.

faq.question: Does it contain live market data?
faq.answer: No. It contains reference documentation. Live data requires separate network access to Polymarket services.

faq.question: Is it safe to share private keys with it?
faq.answer: No. Never paste private keys, seed phrases, API secrets, or passphrases into prompts or shared files.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-focused, but it covers authenticated trading, private keys, .env files, and external APIs.
